events.label: assistant
events.text: I’ll write the structured finding directly to `/app/answer.json`, treating the observed T-tap as a supervision/code compliance fault for an in-service fire alarm circuit.

events.label: shell
events.text:
/bin/sh -lc "cat > /app/answer.json <<'EOF'
{
  \"decision\": \"fail\",
  \"risk\": \"high\",
  \"work_stage\": \"in-service\",
  \"component_conditions\": [\"installed-defective\", \"non-compliant\", \"degraded\", \"failed\"],
  \"percent_complete\": 100,
  \"value\": 0,
  \"sound_source\": \"\",
  \"confidence\": 0.86,
  \"event_time\": 0.0,
  \"rate\": 0.0,
  \"order\": [],
  \"workable\": [],
  \"findings\": [
    \"Class B fire alarm circuit has an observed T-tap/branch connection that can leave part of the circuit unsupervised.\",
    \"An open on the tapped branch may not be detected at the control unit if the end-of-line supervision remains intact on the main run.\",
    \"In-service fire alarm initiating, signaling, or notification devices on the branch may fail to report or operate while the panel shows normal supervision.\"
  ],
  \"actions\": [
    \"Treat the affected fire alarm circuit as impaired and follow the facility fire alarm impairment procedure until corrected.\",
    \"Trace and document the circuit routing, devices, splice points, and end-of-line location against approved fire alarm drawings and submittals.\",
    \"Remove the improper T-tap or reconfigure the pathway using listed equipment and a wiring method permitted by the manufacturer, approved design, and NFPA 72 pathway requirements.\",
    \"Retest circuit supervision, trouble reporting, alarm operation, and affected devices after correction, then obtain acceptance by the authority having jurisdiction or third-party inspector as required.\"
  ],
  \"rationale\": \"The most likely failure mode is loss of required pathway supervision caused by an unauthorized branch on a Class B fire alarm circuit. Class B supervision depends on the circuit being arranged so opens, grounds, and device faults are detected by the fire alarm control unit. A T-tap can bypass that supervision, so an open branch or disconnected devices may not produce a trouble condition while leaving fire alarm coverage impaired. Because the system is already in service, this is a life-safety non-compliance requiring corrective work and retesting.\",
  \"references\": [
    \"NFPA 72 - Protected premises fire alarm systems pathway supervision requirements\",
    \"NFPA 72 - Class B pathway performance and trouble signal requirements\",
    \"NFPA 72 - Inspection, testing, and maintenance requirements for fire alarm system circuits and devices\",
    \"NFPA 72 - Installation in accordance with approved documentatio…
